privacySections.label: Connection — Local First
items: Conversations travel only between this device and your configured OpenClaw gateway. ClawBoy does not proxy, relay, or read your traffic.
Connections use encrypted TLS (wss://) by default. If you enter a ws:// or http:// URL, the app will warn you before saving.

TOFU SPKI certificate pinning is supported. After the first successful connection, ClawBoy records the public key of your gateway's certificate. You can activate it as a live pin under Settings → Connection → Pinned Key. Once pinned, a certificate mismatch will block the connection before any credentials are sent.

To minimise the risk of TLS-inspecting proxies and rogue certificate authorities, we recommend enabling gateway certificate pinning and accessing your gateway via Tailscale, WireGuard, or a Cloudflare Tunnel.

privacySections.label: What Is Stored on Device
items: The gateway token, device identity, and chat-cache encryption key are stored in the iOS Keychain / Android Keystore via Expo SecureStore.
To show chats instantly on cold launch, the most recent 20 messages per server are encrypted with AES-256-GCM before being written to disk. The encryption key lives in SecureStore.
Server configuration names and URLs, theme, and UI preferences are stored in plaintext app storage and contain no credentials.

privacySections.label: Device Identity
items: A unique Ed25519 key pair is generated on-device the first time you launch the app.
The private key never leaves this device. Only the resulting signature is sent to your gateway during the connection challenge.

privacySections.label: Feedback & Diagnostics
items: Nothing is sent outside the app unless you tap "Report a Problem / Feature Request".

When you submit, your message and optional diagnostic info (app version, build number, OS, device model, locale) are sent to a Cloudflare Worker operated by this team and used to create a public GitHub Issue.
Screenshots you attach are uploaded to a public GitHub repository for embedding in the Issue. Treat them as public — do not attach screenshots containing sensitive information.
Diagnostics never include your gateway URL, token, or message content.

privacySections.label: Over-the-Air (OTA) Updates
items: New JavaScript bundles are downloaded from Expo's update servers and verified with code-signing before execution.
This is the only routine network request the app makes outside of your gateway.

privacySections.label: What We Don't Do
items: No off-device analytics, telemetry, or behavioural tracking.
No third-party crash-reporting service (no Sentry, no Bugsnag).
No ad SDKs, no device fingerprinting.
No remote code execution beyond signed Expo OTA bundles.
No silent re-pairing — if the device identity is rejected, the app stops and asks what you want to do.

threatSections.label: Protections ClawBoy Provides Today
items: Connections use encrypted TLS (wss://) by default. The app warns strongly if you enter a ws:// or http:// URL.
The gateway token, the device's Ed25519 private key, and the chat-cache encryption key are stored in the iOS Keychain / Android Keystore (Expo SecureStore) and never enter plain app storage.
A unique Ed25519 key pair is generated on-device and never exported; only its signature is transmitted, and only to your gateway.
Per-device pairing requires explicit manual approval on the gateway side. A stolen Bearer token alone cannot complete pairing on a new device — the private key is also required.
ClawBoy does not proxy, relay, or log your traffic; conversations flow directly between this device and your configured gateway.
No analytics SDK, no crash reporting, no third-party telemetry.

threatSections.label: Threats That Are Well Mitigated
items: Passive eavesdropping on a hostile Wi‑Fi network (covered by TLS).
ISP or network-layer snooping (covered by TLS + direct connection to your server).
Reuse of a stolen Bearer token — the device identity key pair prevents a new device from authenticating with a token alone; leaking the token is not sufficient.
Session hijacking on an unpaired device — the handshake requires a valid Ed25519 signature that only this device can produce.
Accidental insecure transport — saving a ws:// URL requires explicit user confirmation.

When certificate pinning is enabled: TLS-inspecting corporate proxies and rogue CAs — ClawBoy records your gateway's certificate public key on first connection. Once you set that key as a pin under Settings → Connection → Pinned Key, the app will block any connection with a mismatched certificate before credentials are sent.
When certificate pinning is enabled: MDM-installed root CAs — the OS CA store is bypassed for that connection.

threatSections.label: Threats Not Fully Mitigated Without Pinning
items: TLS-inspecting corporate proxies. Enterprise TLS-inspection appliances (Zscaler, Netskope, Palo Alto, etc.) present a CA-signed certificate trusted by the OS; without pinning, it is indistinguishable from a real connection.
MDM-installed root CAs. Managed devices may have additional CAs installed by IT that can sign for any hostname.
Rogue or compromised public certificate authorities. The public CA ecosystem has many issuers; a compromised or coerced CA could issue a seemingly valid certificate for your gateway hostname.
Nation-state adversaries with the ability to coerce a CA.
Enable certificate pinning under Settings → Connection → Pinned Key to close these gaps.

threatSections.label: What You Can Do Now
items: Enable certificate pinning. ClawBoy records your gateway's certificate public key on first connection — go to Settings → Connection → Pinned Key and tap Pin to enforce it.
Run your gateway behind Tailscale, WireGuard, or a Cloudflare Tunnel for an extra layer of protection that reduces exposure to proxy-in-the-middle paths on the public internet.
Avoid installing ClawBoy on managed/enterprise devices where you don't control the MDM configuration, unless pinning is enabled.
When approving a new device on your gateway, verify that the device fingerprint shown in the ClawBoy pairing UI matches what the gateway reports.
If you suspect your gateway token has been compromised, rotate it immediately.
Keep both the app and your OpenClaw gateway up to date.

threatSections.label: What We Will Never Do
items: Proxy, relay, or read your conversations.
Log message content off-device.
Integrate ad, analytics, or device-fingerprinting SDKs.
Execute code from remote sources (except signed Expo OTA bundles).
Store credentials in plaintext app storage.

sections.title: Added
items: Native iOS/Android chat client for **OpenClaw** over WebSocket (`wss://`) with JSON-RPC request/response/event framing, keepalive ticks, and defensive gateway payload parsing.
**Device pairing** via an **Ed25519 device identity key** held exclusively in SecureStore; challenge–response handshake required before connecting.
**Reconnection**: exponential back-off, jitter, attempt cap, and automatic reconnect on app foreground.
**Session stream isolation**: switching sessions during streaming doesn't corrupt the UI; active-stream guard on chat events.
Optional **SPKI certificate pinning** on iOS and Android via the `expo-pinned-websocket` native module; TOFU discovery, pin-mismatch handling, and a settings UI for managing gateway pins.
**Streaming assistant replies** with expandable thinking/reasoning blocks and tool-call cards (pending, running, completed, error).
**Safe Markdown rendering** of assistant content (no raw HTML injection).
**Session management**: list, create, switch, and reset sessions; slide-out session list with previews.
**Agent** and **model** selectors wired to the gateway catalogue.
**Slash-command palette** (`/`) with server-side suggestions and **command confirmation** when the gateway requires it.
**Multi-server configuration**: labelled gateways; gateway tokens stored exclusively in SecureStore (never written to AsyncStorage in plaintext).
**Connection status** and banners (connecting, pairing required, identity rejected, error, etc.).

**Settings**: server block (add/edit/remove profiles, logs), appearance (theme mode and dark variants), media options, **text-to-speech** controls (auto-speak replies, device- and gateway-voice preferences), and an **About** page with in-app changelog.
**Onboarding** flow when no server is configured.

Optional **ClawBoy cloud identity** (Supabase): sign in with Apple, Google (OAuth), and **email magic-link**; accounts row in settings is nullable and doesn't block pure-local gateway use; Supabase session tokens in SecureStore.

**Feedback** submission to a configurable proxy endpoint with optional **recent screenshot** attachment, privacy-conscious client-side handling, and a **Cloudflare Worker** companion for triage (see `infra/feedback-worker/`).
**Encrypted disk chat cache** (AES-GCM) for fast cold-launch; encryption key in SecureStore.
**OTA update** integration (`expo-updates`) with a critical-update modal when required.

Optional Supabase schema **infrastructure-as-code** with Edge Functions (`infra/supabase/`) — accounts, entitlement placeholders, `account-delete`, founders schema, a **`purchases-webhook`** for store events — plus timestamped migrations and a local `supabase/config.toml` CLI workflow; see `infra/supabase/README.md`.
**Unit tests** (Jest) for protocol helpers and key UI components; test config and mocks aligned with Expo / React Native.
**Internationalisation** (`i18n`): English and Simplified Chinese (`zh-CN`) copy bundles with `LanguageProvider` and wiring across onboarding, chat, input, settings, and common UI.
**Demo mode**: local scripted OpenClaw client, demo storage, in-chat demo banner, and an onboarding path for exploring the UI without a real gateway.

In-app purchases via `react-native-purchases` (**RevenueCat**): `PurchasesContext`, product helpers, and a settings block for **Founder** support and optional **tip jar**; Founder badge displayed for eligible accounts.
**`serverPointers`** helpers and related Supabase types for optional cloud-assisted server profile discovery.
**`ServerProfileSyncContext`**: optional sync of server profile metadata with Supabase (includes pointer-resolution unit tests).
**Account settings** screen and a richer **Accounts** block (sign-in, account management) alongside the existing Supabase auth flow.
**Settings pairing card** for device-pairing status and actions; in-chat **audio playing pill** for inline playback / TTS.
**`useThrottledValue`** for UIs that should not re-render on every frame; additional **hooks/utilities** including onboarding-flow snapshot tests.

sections.title: Changed
items: Iterative refactors of the OpenClaw WebSocket **client** and **chat** modules for cleaner stream handling, typed events, reconnection behaviour, and alignment with gateway semantics.
**UI polish** across chat (header, banners, bubbles, list), onboarding, settings (including server block and meta panel), and theme tokens toward a unified design language.
**Pairing required** prompt moved from a standalone chat-screen card to **Settings** (removed `PairingRequiredCard` from chat; added pairing card in settings).
Updated **message list/bubble**, **slash-command palette**, **tool and thinking cards**, **connection banner**, and **input bar** for demo-mode, i18n, media/audio cues, and layout consistency.
Split **Jest** into **logic** and **components** projects with tweaked shared setup for faster, better-structured runs.

Expanded **`useServerConfig`** and related settings flows to support multi-profile editing, sync, and the server-row experience; updated **`infra/supabase/README`** to reflect new migrations and functions.

sections.title: Security
items: Gateway tokens, device identity material, active Supabase session tokens, and chat-cache keys stored via **`expo-secure-store`** rather than AsyncStorage; Supabase cloud tables use RLS-oriented schema with documentation.
Certificate pinning and explicit handling when the gateway certificate no longer matches the stored pin.
